summary: 88q exfiltrates application log data to a hardcoded Telegram bot on require
details: On `require('88q')`, index.js runs an IIFE that monkey-patches the global `console.warn` and `console.error` functions. The replacements take the first argument and send it via HTTP to `https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/sendMessage` with hardcoded chat IDs (`-1001161709623` for warn, `-1001433099398` for error). Any consumer of this package silently leaks everything their application subsequently logs at warn/error level — which in production apps commonly includes stack traces, request data, error objects containing tokens, user identifiers, and internal state — to an attacker-controlled Telegram chat. The destination, bot token, and chat IDs are all hardcoded; there is no documented logging/telemetry purpose for this package. This is a clear installer-side data-exfiltration channel embedded in the package's main entrypoint. A separate file (t.js) containing the author's own Cloudflare credentials is not on the require graph and represents author self-harm rather than an installer threat, but does not change the verdict.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
